news_summary: Advancements in environmental science and sustainable living are highlighted by innovations in cloud research, recognition of key contributors to Earth observation, California's new building environmental scoring, and widespread adoption of solar panels reducing electricity costs.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine des sciences de l'environnement et du mode de vie durable sont mis en avant par les innovations en matière de recherche sur les nuages, la reconnaissance des principaux acteurs de l'observation de la Terre, le nouveau système californien d'évaluation environnementale des bâtiments et l'adoption généralisée des panneaux solaires, qui permettent de réduire les coûts d'électricité.
news_summary_es: Los avances en las ciencias ambientales y el estilo de vida sostenible se ponen de manifiesto en las innovaciones en la investigación sobre las nubes, el reconocimiento a las figuras clave de la observación de la Tierra, el nuevo sistema de puntuación medioambiental de los edificios de California y la generalización del uso de paneles solares para reducir los costes de la electricidad.

articles.title: Jim Irons, Former Landsat Project Scientist, Wins Pecora Award
articles.summary: Landsat’s Jim Irons won the prestigious William T. Pecora Award. Irons, now an emeritus scientist at NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, played an integral role in shaping the Landsat program into what it is today.
articles.content:
Now an emeritus scientist at NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Dr. Jim Irons is the former Landsat 8 Project and GSFC Earth Science Division Director.

Last month, Landsat’s very own Jim Irons won the prestigious William T. Pecora Award.

Irons, now an emeritus scientist at NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, played an integral role in shaping the Landsat program into what it is today. He served as deputy project scientist for Landsat 7 before taking over as project scientist for Landsat 8. From the earliest days of Landsat 8—then called the Landsat Data Continuity Mission (LDCM)—all the way through launch and operation, Irons worked across the agency and with colleagues at the USGS to ensure that Landsat continued providing critical data to researchers around the world. He championed rigorous calibration standards and fought to keep the thermal band on Landsat 8. Now, with projects like OpenET relying on evapotranspiration data derived from Landsat thermal imagery, the strength of his vision has only become more apparent.

Irons also served as the director of NASA Goddard’s Earth Science Division during the turbulent early days of the COVID-19 pandemic. Contending with global disruption, he prioritized making sure that everyone had the support that they needed to continue doing great work. As a leader and a scientist, Irons left a legacy of collaboration and innovation that lives on today.

We checked in with Irons about his role in Landsat’s history, what it takes to be a good leader, and winning the Pecora award:

NASA missions are so collaborative. Are there mentors, colleagues, or teams that you would want to share this recognition with or give special mention to?

One reason I feel so honored is that prior recipients have been my supervisors, mentors, role models, and colleagues whose work I admired and who inspired me. There's a long list of people who have been recipients, and I am very honored to be added to that list.

There are also many people who have not yet been recognized who are very deserving. I've written letters of support for others, and I hope I'm called on again because there are more people who deserve recognition than there are awards to give out.

One of the things highlighted in the Pecora Award announcement was your commitment to the long-term continuous data record of Landsat. Looking at the Landsat program, why is this continuity so critical for Earth science today?

Data continuity is the backbone of the Landsat program. We are looking for change over time. When we talk about climate change and the impact of humans on the land surface, those changes are multi-decadal. We wouldn't be able to understand, characterize, and monitor those changes without a continuous data record.

And it's really important that the data record is well-calibrated. When we see changes between data from one Landsat sensor relative to another, we need to be confident that it's a change occurring on the Earth, not a change in the performance of the sensors.

That's another major contribution cited in your award: how much you pushed for rigorous data calibration and quality assurance. How did you establish those processes, and how did that make Landsat the gold standard of satellite data?

Early in my career, I got in trouble over calibration. NASA was flying an airborne sensor called the Thematic Mapper Simulator, intended to anticipate the capabilities of Landsat 4 and 5. But the operators kept changing the radiometric gain in-flight to maximize the dynamic range. I told NASA Headquarters that we couldn't compare that data to the actual Thematic Mappers if they kept changing the gain—it wasn't the same radiometry! The HQ manager got really upset, but I weathered the storm and stuck to my guns.

Later, when Landsat 4 and 5 were returned to the U.S. government from private operation, there had been no real calibration since launch. I advocated for a ground system component at USGS EROS to perform calibration. I didn't build it, but I did advocate for USGS to hire a brilliant guy named Jim Storey, who developed the software for the precise geolocation of pixels in the data.

When I became Landsat 8 Project Scientist, we needed a pre-launch calibration lead. I advocated for Brian Markham. Brian just did a remarkable job ensuring the calibration of the Operational Land Imager (OLI) and its cross-calibration with previous instruments. He was modest, humble, and built a highly effective team across private industry and agencies.

Another important part of your legacy was the effort to ensure that thermal-infrared measurements continued onto Landsat 8. Why was retaining those measurements so important?

Back when USGS charged for data, the use of thermal data was minimal. Some well-respected papers even claimed it wouldn't be possible to use thermal data to estimate evapotranspiration rates. Based on that, the Director of Earth Sciences at NASA HQ was convinced that the thermal capability wasn't providing a return on investment.

But while this debate was ongoing, people began developing methodologies for estimating evapotranspiration and water consumption using thermal data—prominently Martha Anderson at the USDA, and researchers at the University of Idaho. It became crucial for monitoring agricultural water use in the West, and was even used in adjudicating water rights. It was also useful for cloud detection and fire monitoring.

I felt strongly that dropping the thermal capability was inconsistent with our directive to continue the Landsat data record. However, due to time pressures and budget constraints, the decision was initially made to fly Landsat 8 without a thermal instrument. But then, when our schedule was pushed back by an independent review board, a window opened up. Center Director Ed Weiler, who had moved to HQ, supported putting a thermal sensor on the payload. Kathy Richardson and engineer Fernando Pellerano were assigned to build it on an incredibly tight schedule, and they did an unbelievable job.

Now, deriving evapotranspiration rates for water consumption is considered essential. Ironically, for Landsat 9, NASA HQ even briefly considered launching a satellite with only a thermal sensor!

You were the Project Scientist from the earliest days of the Landsat Data Continuity Mission (LDCM) all the way through the Landsat 8 launch and beyond. What was the biggest challenge you faced during its development?

There were a lot of problems. Laughs. Because of the Land Remote Sensing Policy Act of 1992, the government was exploring commercial data buys for the follow-on mission. NASA spent five painful years attempting to implement LDCM as a commercial data buy. Only one company ultimately responded to the RFP, and it wasn't a good deal for NASA, so it was rejected.

Then we were directed to put the Landsat sensor on an NPOESS platform (combining civilian and military weather satellite requirements). That platform wasn't technically suitable, and the program ultimately fell apart.

Finally, the Office of Science and Technology Policy directed us to launch a free-flyer. Bill Ochs took over as project manager, and he deserves so much credit for the success of Landsat 8. He essentially rescued the project and put it on a path to success.

Reflecting on the partnership between USGS and NASA, how did you help build that, and what makes long-term interagency collaboration possible?

Darrell Williams and I worked very hard to establish a good relationship between NASA Goddard and USGS EROS. I took many trips to Sioux Falls. With Landsat 7, the EROS Center Director at the time, Don Lauer, brought in new people with great experience, like Jim Storey, Doug Daniels, and Jim Nelson. They developed the geometric rectification software for Landsat 7, and by the time we worked on Landsat 8, they had the right people in place to develop the whole data processing system. And we all got along really well with them. We still keep in touch with a number of them and consider them friends.

With Landsat 10 on the horizon, are there emerging applications or discoveries you're excited about?

Yes. A major emerging capability is using Landsat data in concert with other systems, like ESA's Sentinel-2, or with LIDAR and radar for 3D forest mapping. The community has asked for more frequent observations, especially more frequent thermal observations to measure water consumption more precisely without extrapolating over long gaps during the growing season.

There's also great interest in using Landsat for water quality assessment, combining it with the PACE mission to monitor coastal and inland water quality. And tracking glacial velocities, glacial retreat, and even population displacement in conflict regions are all expanding areas. Landsat is truly foundational.

What was your biggest takeaway about leadership from your role as Director for the Earth Science Division at Goddard?

I was asked to step up after my predecessor, Piers Sellers—who was an absolute superstar—passed away. My main goal was simply to create an environment where the highly diverse researchers within the division could be successful. I wanted to minimize bureaucratic hindrances so they could focus on their work.

What I learned is that there is a limit to authority. Dictating doesn't work. You have to lead, engage people, bring them into discussions, and get their buy-in. I used to joke that the job was like working with 1,400 valedictorians! It's a high-achieving, dedicated group. My challenge was sometimes just reminding them to respect the work of the person down the hall, because people can get so fiercely focused on their own research.

My primary goal during my tenure was to provide stability, especially since it spanned what was then the longest government shutdown in history, followed by the COVID-19 pandemic. I was incredibly impressed by how productive the division remained through a complete disruption in how they worked.

What is the most important piece of advice you would give to young scientists?

Persistence. Persistence in pursuing your interests is critical. The only reason Landsat 8 was a success was that we persisted through several failed attempts to reformulate the program, schedule challenges, and budget uncertainties.

Funding and mission success aren't entitlements based on your name or reputation. You have to work hard, keep putting forward proposals, do good work, and persist through rejections. If you really believe in what you're doing, Goddard is a great place to work. You can get a lot done. But it takes persistence.

This interview was condensed and lightly edited for clarity.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant and positive impact of Dr. Jim Irons' leadership and scientific contributions to the Landsat program, which provides critical, continuous, and well-calibrated Earth observation data essential for climate science, water management, and environmental monitoring worldwide. The story emphasizes broad societal benefits through improved understanding of climate change, water use, and land surface changes, supported by detailed context about the challenges overcome and innovations achieved.
articles.personality_title: Jim Irons honored for leading Landsat’s vital Earth observation work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Landsat is a NASA program that uses satellites to take pictures of Earth. These pictures help scientists study changes in the environment over many years. Keeping this data accurate and continuous is very important for understanding things like climate change and water use.

**What happened** – Dr. Jim Irons, a scientist who helped lead the Landsat program, won the William T. Pecora Award last month. He worked at NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and was the project scientist for Landsat 8. He made sure the satellite data stayed high quality and that important instruments, like the thermal sensor, stayed on the satellite.

**Impact** – Irons’ work helped Landsat become a trusted source for Earth data used worldwide. His push for careful calibration means scientists can trust changes they see in the images are real changes on Earth. Keeping the thermal sensor was key for measuring water use in farming and detecting fires. These tools support important decisions about water rights and environmental health.

**What’s next step** – Landsat will continue to improve, working with other satellites to provide more frequent and detailed Earth images. New uses include 3D forest mapping and tracking glaciers and water quality. This ongoing work will help scientists monitor the planet even better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dr. Jim Irons’ leadership and dedication have made the Landsat program a trusted, long-lasting source of Earth data vital for studying our changing planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: Jim Irons récompensé pour son rôle clé dans l’observation terrestre de Landsat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Landsat est un programme de la NASA qui utilise des satellites pour prendre des images de la Terre. Ces images aident les scientifiques à étudier les changements environnementaux sur de nombreuses années. Il est très important que ces données soient précises et continues pour comprendre le changement climatique et l’utilisation de l’eau.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Dr Jim Irons, un scientifique qui a dirigé le programme Landsat, a reçu en avril le prix William T. Pecora. Il travaillait au Centre spatial Goddard de la NASA et était le responsable scientifique de Landsat 8. Il a veillé à la qualité des données satellites et à la présence d’instruments importants, comme le capteur thermique.

**Impact** – Le travail de Jim Irons a fait de Landsat une source fiable de données sur la Terre utilisée dans le monde entier. Son insistance sur une calibration rigoureuse permet aux scientifiques de savoir que les changements observés sont réels. Le maintien du capteur thermique est essentiel pour mesurer la consommation d’eau en agriculture et détecter les incendies, aidant à la gestion de l’eau et à la santé environnementale.

**Prochaine étape** – Landsat continuera de s’améliorer en travaillant avec d’autres satellites pour fournir des images de la Terre plus fréquentes et détaillées. De nouvelles applications incluent la cartographie 3D des forêts, le suivi des glaciers et la qualité de l’eau. Ce travail aidera encore mieux à surveiller la planète.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le leadership et la persévérance du Dr Jim Irons ont fait de Landsat un programme fiable et durable, essentiel pour étudier notre planète en évolution.
articles.personality_title_es: Jim Irons reconocido por su liderazgo en la observación terrestre de Landsat
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Landsat es un programa de la NASA que utiliza satélites para tomar imágenes de la Tierra. Estas imágenes ayudan a los científicos a estudiar cambios en el medio ambiente a lo largo de muchos años. Es muy importante que estos datos sean precisos y continuos para entender el cambio climático y el uso del agua.

**Qué pasó** – El Dr. Jim Irons, un científico que dirigió el programa Landsat, recibió el Premio William T. Pecora el mes pasado. Trabajó en el Centro Espacial Goddard de la NASA y fue científico del proyecto de Landsat 8. Se aseguró de que los datos del satélite fueran de alta calidad y que instrumentos importantes, como el sensor térmico, permanecieran en el satélite.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Irons ayudó a que Landsat sea una fuente confiable de datos sobre la Tierra usados en todo el mundo. Su insistencia en una calibración rigurosa permite a los científicos confiar en que los cambios que ven son reales. Mantener el sensor térmico fue clave para medir el uso del agua en la agricultura y detectar incendios, apoyando decisiones importantes sobre agua y medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Landsat seguirá mejorando, trabajando con otros satélites para ofrecer imágenes más frecuentes y detalladas de la Tierra. Nuevos usos incluyen mapas 3D de bosques y el seguimiento de glaciares y calidad del agua. Este trabajo ayudará a monitorear el planeta aún mejor.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El liderazgo y la dedicación del Dr. Jim Irons han convertido a Landsat en una fuente confiable y duradera de datos vitales para estudiar nuestro planeta cambiante.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized satellite gently orbiting above a vibrant Earth, with layered glowing bands symbolizing continuous data streams flowing from the satellite to a collaborative network of abstract interconnected shapes representing teamwork and rigorous scientific calibration.

articles.title: NASA’s CloudCube Pioneers Miniaturized Radar to Study Clouds, Precipitation
articles.summary: A compact, multifrequency radar built by a team at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory will make it easier to collect information about dynamic cloud systems. Called CloudCube, this new instrument simultaneously probes the atmosphere with three radar signals, spanning 36 to 240 GHz, for optimized sensitivity to a wide range of water droplet and ice particle sizes.
articles.content:
A compact, multifrequency radar built by a team at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory will make it easier to collect information about dynamic cloud systems. Called CloudCube, this new instrument simultaneously probes the atmosphere with three radar signals, spanning 36 to 240 GHz, for optimized sensitivity to a wide range of water droplet and ice particle sizes.

Figure 1: A prototype of CloudCube’s G-band channel was installed at Cape Grim, Tasmania, as a guest instrument for the Department of Energy’s Cloud and Precipitation Experiment at Kennaook (CAPE-K) Credit: Raquel Rodriguez Monje / JPL

Built with funding from NASA’s Earth Science Technology Office Instrument Incubator Program, CloudCube transmits and receives Ka-, W-, and G-band signals, making it the first compact radar system capable of simultaneously probing meteorological targets at wavelengths spanning approximately one to ten millimeters. Researchers will be able to combine information from the three signals to learn more about the initiation and evolution of precipitation, as well as cloud microphysics and radiative properties.

“We’re making a low-power, low-mass instrument to facilitate new cost-efficient missions for atmospheric observations. Building a multi-frequency radar, especially at G-band, is very novel,” said Raquel Rodriguez Monje, a systems engineer at JPL and principal investigator for CloudCube.

Each of CloudCube’s three signals observes a different element of cloud physics. Ka-band radar signals are ideal for collecting precipitation profiles; W-band radar signals are preferred for measuring cloud particles that give rise to precipitation; and G-band radar signals, which have never been collected from a space-based instrument, are ideal for measuring ice and liquid water content inside very light clouds (a paper describing this measurement can be found here).

Probing the atmosphere simultaneously with three signals allows researchers to collect data on all these cloud features at once, which is valuable for improving weather forecasts and especially climate modeling. CloudCube leverages innovations in millimeter-wave hardware to pack three radar modules–one for each signal–within a single compact system.

Figure 2. A photo of the radar electronics for CloudCube’s compact G-band radar. Producing G-band radar signals requires a large amount of energy, and CloudCube is one of the first instruments to produce those signals effectively from a compact platform. Credit: Raquel Rodriguez Monje / NASA JPL

One CloudCube innovation concerns the specialized components required to transmit G-band power from a compact, low-power instrument. The detection of cloud signals requires high transmit power, which CloudCube achieves by combining the outputs of multiple high-efficiency frequency-multiplication devices that allow the instrument to generate hundreds of milliWatts at 240 GHz. Another innovation of CloudCube is that it was designed to use as few radio frequency components as possible to reduce its mass and power consumption, which could lower the cost of future Earth-observing orbital instruments.

Flying an instrument equipped with G-band radar in space will be a new capability and will allow researchers to achieve greater spatial resolution and sensitivity in the study of cloud microphysical processes.

“Basically, we’re weighing clouds using these combinations of frequencies in a way that we couldn’t do before we had the G-band,” said Matt Lebsock, a researcher at JPL and co-investigator for CloudCube.

The instrument has been tested in the field. A ground-based prototype of CloudCube’s G-band channel operated continuously for 11 months during the Department of Energy’s Cloud and Precipitation Experiment at Kennaook (CAPE-K) campaign. CloudCube also participated in the Eastern Pacific Cloud Aerosol Precipitation Experiment, a ground campaign sponsored by the Department of Energy. A paper describing the results of that experiment can be found here.

Most recently, CloudCube successfully operated all three frequency bands from NASA’s Gulfstream III aircraft and collected its first airborne observations of snowfall as part of the North American Upstream Feature-Resolving and Tropopause Uncertainty Reconnaissance Experiment campaign—a NASA-funded campaign designed to improve forecasts of high-impact winter weather. The CloudCube team is currently calibrating and processing the data for public release.

For additional details, see the entry for this project on NASA TechPort.

Project Lead: Dr. Raquel Rodriguez Monje, NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory

Sponsoring Organization: NASA’s Earth Science Technology Office Instrument Incubation Program
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel compact radar system, CloudCube, developed by NASA to improve atmospheric observations by simultaneously probing cloud microphysics and precipitation with three radar frequencies. This technological breakthrough enables better data collection for weather forecasting and climate modeling, which has broad and meaningful environmental impact. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights significant innovation with potential large-scale benefits for society through improved understanding of clouds and precipitation.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s CloudCube radar offers new insights into clouds and precipitation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Understanding clouds and precipitation is important for weather forecasts and climate studies. Traditional radar systems that study clouds can be large and use only one frequency at a time, limiting the detail they capture.

**What happened** – NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory created a small radar system called CloudCube that uses three different radar signals at once. These signals, ranging from 36 to 240 GHz, help measure different parts of clouds, like water droplets and ice particles. CloudCube has been tested on the ground and from a NASA aircraft, where it collected data on snowfall during a recent weather campaign.

**Impact** – CloudCube’s ability to use three radar frequencies together in a compact design is new and important. It can provide more detailed information about how clouds form and produce rain or snow. This helps scientists improve weather forecasts and climate models. Also, CloudCube uses less power and weighs less than older radars, making it easier and cheaper to send into space.

**What’s next step** – Scientists are now processing the data collected by CloudCube and will share it publicly. The team hopes to use this technology in future space missions to study clouds worldwide with better detail and accuracy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s CloudCube radar uses three frequencies in a small device to better study clouds and precipitation, improving weather and climate science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le radar CloudCube de la NASA offre de nouvelles informations sur les nuages et les précipitations
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Comprendre les nuages et les précipitations est important pour les prévisions météorologiques et les études climatiques. Les radars traditionnels qui étudient les nuages sont souvent grands et n’utilisent qu’une seule fréquence à la fois, ce qui limite les détails capturés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Laboratoire de propulsion à réaction (JPL) de la NASA a créé un petit système radar appelé CloudCube qui utilise trois signaux radar différents en même temps. Ces signaux, allant de 36 à 240 GHz, aident à mesurer différentes parties des nuages, comme les gouttes d’eau et les particules de glace. CloudCube a été testé au sol puis à bord d’un avion de la NASA, où il a collecté des données sur la neige lors d’une récente campagne météo.

**Impact** – La capacité de CloudCube à utiliser trois fréquences radar simultanément dans un design compact est nouvelle et importante. Il peut fournir des informations plus détaillées sur la formation des nuages et la production de pluie ou de neige. Cela aide les scientifiques à améliorer les prévisions météorologiques et les modèles climatiques. De plus, CloudCube consomme moins d’énergie et est plus léger que les radars plus anciens, ce qui facilite et réduit le coût de son envoi dans l’espace.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques traitent maintenant les données collectées par CloudCube et vont les partager publiquement. L’équipe espère utiliser cette technologie dans de futures missions spatiales pour étudier les nuages dans le monde entier avec plus de précision.

**Phrase clé** – Le radar CloudCube de la NASA utilise trois fréquences dans un petit appareil pour mieux étudier les nuages et les précipitations, améliorant la science météorologique et climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: El radar CloudCube de la NASA ofrece nuevas perspectivas sobre las nubes y las precipitaciones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Entender las nubes y las precipitaciones es importante para los pronósticos del tiempo y los estudios climáticos. Los radares tradicionales que estudian las nubes suelen ser grandes y solo usan una frecuencia a la vez, lo que limita el detalle que pueden capturar.

**Qué pasó** – El Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro (JPL) de la NASA creó un pequeño sistema de radar llamado CloudCube que usa tres señales de radar diferentes al mismo tiempo. Estas señales, que van de 36 a 240 GHz, ayudan a medir diferentes partes de las nubes, como gotas de agua y partículas de hielo. CloudCube se probó en tierra y desde un avión de la NASA, donde recogió datos sobre la nieve durante una campaña reciente.

**Impacto** – La capacidad de CloudCube para usar tres frecuencias de radar juntas en un diseño compacto es nueva e importante. Puede proporcionar información más detallada sobre cómo se forman las nubes y producen lluvia o nieve. Esto ayuda a los científicos a mejorar los pronósticos del tiempo y los modelos climáticos. Además, CloudCube usa menos energía y pesa menos que los radares antiguos, lo que facilita y abarata su envío al espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están procesando los datos recogidos por CloudCube y los compartirán públicamente. El equipo espera usar esta tecnología en futuras misiones espaciales para estudiar las nubes en todo el mundo con mayor detalle y precisión.

**Resumen en una frase** – El radar CloudCube de la NASA usa tres frecuencias en un dispositivo pequeño para estudiar mejor las nubes y las precipitaciones, mejorando la ciencia del clima y el tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a compact, futuristic radar device emitting three distinct, softly glowing waves in shades of blue, purple, and light gray, gently probing layered, stylized clouds filled with delicate ice crystals and water droplets, set against a serene sky at dawn with subtle atmospheric gradients, emphasizing innovation and the study of cloud microphysics.

articles.title: California Just Put Its Buildings on an Environmental Scoreboard
articles.summary:
Every year, California’s commercial and apartment buildings burn through 109 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity, guzzle...
The post California Just Put Its Buildings on an Environmental Scoreboard appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Every year, California’s commercial and apartment buildings burn through 109 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity, guzzle 240 billion gallons of water, and release 23 million metric tons of carbon — and until now, almost none of that was easy for the public to see in one place.

That changed on May 28, when Measurabl and U.S. Green Building Council of California (UCGBC California) launched the California Building Performance Pulse, a free public dashboard that tracks how the state’s commercial and multifamily buildings perform on energy, carbon, and water. It covers more than 1.3 billion square feet of floor space across six years of utility data, one of the largest public windows into California building performance yet, and lets anyone compare buildings by city, property type, floor area, and year built.

Data for Decision-Making

The California Air Resources Board attributes roughly a quarter of the state’s greenhouse gas emissions to residential and commercial buildings once electricity use, on-site fuel combustion, and refrigerant leaks are counted together. On-site fossil gas combustion alone accounts for about 10 percent of the statewide total, and that slice has proven far harder to shrink than emissions from electricity or transportation.

The problem is partly one of visibility. Benchmarking laws have multiplied. California requires owners of larger commercial and multifamily buildings to report energy use annually under state law, and dozens of municipal ordinances layer on top, but the resulting data has been scattered, inconsistent, and hard for owners or the public to act on.

As USGBC California has noted in its compliance guidance, benchmarking by itself doesn’t cut emissions; owners have to act on what the numbers reveal. A building owner who can’t see how their property stacks up against similar ones has little basis for deciding what to fix first.

The Pulse dashboard is designed to close that gap, displaying median annual performance, percentile distributions, year-over-year trends, and geographic patterns across building types including office, multifamily, industrial, hospitality, and retail. The aim, USGBC California CEO Ben Stapleton said when announcing the tool, is to make energy, carbon, and water insights more visible and usable for the owners, operators, and policymakers working to improve performance and strengthen resilience across the state.

The Pulse is powered by Measurabl’s larger data infrastructure, which the company says tracks sustainability data across more than 23 billion square feet in 90-plus countries; the California dataset grows as more owners add their buildings.

A Hard Look at Water Usage

What sets the Pulse apart is water. Measurabl describes it as the only public California dashboard to combine energy, carbon, and water in a single platform. Water has long been the neglected leg of the building-performance stool. Most benchmarking tools and ordinances were built around energy and emissions first.

That matters because water intensity varies enormously by building type. Measurabl reports that in its dataset, hotels use roughly 7 to 10 times more water per square foot than offices. Federal benchmarking data points the same direction: the EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager shows hotels and hospitals exceed 50 gallons per square foot per year, while a typical office building uses closer to 13 to 14 gallons.

A benchmark that treats every building the same misses that an underperforming hotel and an underperforming office are different problems at very different scales.

Median water use intensity by property type

Property type Approx. water use intensity (gal/sq ft/yr) Senior care ~60 Hospitals >50 Hotels >50 Office ~13–14 Source: EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager

California’s water picture also makes the timing important. The state began the 2026 water year in unusually good shape — a wet winter pushed it out of drought entirely for the first time in 25 years by mid-January, according to the Governor’s office. But hydrologists at the California WaterBlog caution that a single wet season doesn’t resolve the state’s structural water stress, and the effects of groundwater and Colorado River overdraft will linger for years. Investments in water efficiency can pay off across both wet years and the dry ones that inevitably follow.

The Dashboard Arrives as Rules Tighten

The dashboard lands at a regulatory inflection point. Under Senate Bill 48, the California Energy Commission is developing a statewide strategy for using benchmarking data to manage building energy use and emissions, with a report due to the legislature in 2026. In February, USGBC California released model building performance standard policy guidance to help cities and counties adopt consistent rules. Building performance standards typically set emissions or efficiency targets that ratchet down over time, with financial penalties for missing them.

For owners, that means the era of simply reporting data is giving way to one when they must meet efficiency targets. Knowing how a building’s performance compares — and which peers are doing better — is the starting point for prioritizing retrofits before compliance deadlines arrive.

At launch, the dataset reflects buildings whose owners participate, and much benchmarking data is self-entered rather than independently verified. A public dashboard is a meaningful step toward transparency, not a complete or audited census of every building in the state.

What You Can Do

If you own or manage a building: Look up how your property type and city perform on the Pulse, then check your own energy and water use against the median. The gap between you and the top quartile is your retrofit roadmap.

Look up how your property type and city perform on the Pulse, then check your own energy and water use against the median. The gap between you and the top quartile is your retrofit roadmap. Don’t ignore water: Especially for hotels, hospitals, multifamily, and senior care, water efficiency is often a lower-cost win than energy retrofits. Towel-and-linen reuse, efficient fixtures, and leak monitoring add up quickly in high-intensity buildings.

Especially for hotels, hospitals, multifamily, and senior care, water efficiency is often a lower-cost win than energy retrofits. Towel-and-linen reuse, efficient fixtures, and leak monitoring add up quickly in high-intensity buildings. Get ahead of the standards: With SB 48 and local building performance standards advancing, treat current benchmarking as preparation for future targets rather than a box to check. Organizing your utility data now makes later compliance far less painful.

With SB 48 and local building performance standards advancing, treat current benchmarking as preparation for future targets rather than a box to check. Organizing your utility data now makes later compliance far less painful. If you’re a tenant or resident: Ask building management how the property benchmarks and whether efficiency upgrades are planned. Demand from occupants is a real driver of building investment.

Ask building management how the property benchmarks and whether efficiency upgrades are planned. Demand from occupants is a real driver of building investment. If you set policy: Public, comparable performance data is the foundation for credible standards. Tools like the Pulse make it easier to design targets grounded in data about how buildings perform rather than on estimates.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the launch of a free public dashboard that tracks energy, carbon, and water usage of California's commercial and multifamily buildings, providing unprecedented transparency and actionable data to help reduce emissions and resource consumption. This initiative supports broad climate action and resource conservation with significant potential impact on statewide environmental sustainability and policy compliance.
articles.personality_title: California launches public dashboard to track building energy, water, and carbon use
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – California’s commercial and apartment buildings use a lot of electricity, water, and produce large amounts of carbon emissions each year. Until now, there was no easy way for the public or building owners to see how these buildings perform in one place.

**What happened** – On May 28, Measurabl and the U.S. Green Building Council of California (USGBC California) introduced the California Building Performance Pulse. This is a free online dashboard that shows energy, carbon, and water use for commercial and multifamily buildings across the state. It includes data from over 1.3 billion square feet of buildings and covers six years of utility information. The dashboard lets anyone compare buildings by city, type, size, and age.

**Impact** – This tool is unique because it combines water use with energy and carbon data, something other public dashboards have not done. Water use varies a lot depending on building type, so including it helps owners understand where to save water as well as energy. For example, hotels use much more water per square foot than offices. The dashboard gives clear, detailed information that building owners, tenants, and policymakers can use to make smarter decisions about upgrades and efficiency. It also arrives as California prepares stricter rules that will require buildings to meet energy and emissions targets.

**What's next step** – Building owners can use the dashboard to see how their properties compare to others and plan improvements before new rules take effect. Tenants can ask their landlords about efficiency plans. Policymakers can use the data to set fair and effective standards. As more owners add their data, the dashboard will grow and become an even stronger tool for improving building performance across California.

**One-sentence takeaway** – California’s new public dashboard offers clear, combined data on building energy, water, and carbon use to help owners and policymakers improve efficiency and meet future environmental goals.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Californie lance un tableau de bord public pour suivre la consommation d’énergie, d’eau et de carbone des bâtiments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les bâtiments commerciaux et résidentiels de Californie consomment beaucoup d’électricité, d’eau et produisent de grandes quantités de carbone chaque année. Jusqu’à présent, il n’était pas facile pour le public ou les propriétaires de voir leurs performances en un seul endroit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 28 mai, Measurabl et le U.S. Green Building Council de Californie (USGBC California) ont lancé le California Building Performance Pulse. Il s’agit d’un tableau de bord en ligne gratuit qui montre la consommation d’énergie, de carbone et d’eau des bâtiments commerciaux et multifamiliaux de l’État. Il inclut des données de plus de 1,3 milliard de pieds carrés de bâtiments et couvre six ans d’informations sur les services publics. Ce tableau de bord permet à chacun de comparer les bâtiments selon la ville, le type, la taille et l’âge.

**Impact** – Cet outil est unique car il combine la consommation d’eau avec les données d’énergie et de carbone, ce que d’autres tableaux de bord publics ne font pas. La consommation d’eau varie beaucoup selon le type de bâtiment, donc l’inclure aide les propriétaires à comprendre où économiser l’eau ainsi que l’énergie. Par exemple, les hôtels utilisent beaucoup plus d’eau par pied carré que les bureaux. Le tableau de bord fournit des informations claires et détaillées que les propriétaires, locataires et décideurs peuvent utiliser pour prendre de meilleures décisions sur les améliorations et l’efficacité. Il arrive aussi alors que la Californie prépare des règles plus strictes exigeant que les bâtiments atteignent des objectifs d’énergie et d’émissions.

**Prochaine étape** – Les propriétaires peuvent utiliser le tableau de bord pour voir comment leurs bâtiments se comparent aux autres et planifier des améliorations avant l’entrée en vigueur des nouvelles règles. Les locataires peuvent demander à leurs gestionnaires des plans d’efficacité. Les décideurs peuvent utiliser ces données pour fixer des normes justes et efficaces. Au fur et à mesure que plus de propriétaires ajoutent leurs données, le tableau de bord grandira et deviendra un outil encore plus puissant pour améliorer la performance des bâtiments en Californie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau tableau de bord public de Californie offre des données claires et combinées sur la consommation d’énergie, d’eau et de carbone des bâtiments pour aider propriétaires et décideurs à améliorer l’efficacité et atteindre les objectifs environnementaux futurs.
articles.personality_title_es: California lanza un panel público para monitorear el uso de energía, agua y carbono en edificios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los edificios comerciales y de apartamentos en California usan mucha electricidad, agua y producen grandes cantidades de carbono cada año. Hasta ahora, no había una manera fácil para que el público o los dueños de edificios vieran su desempeño en un solo lugar.

**Qué pasó** – El 28 de mayo, Measurabl y el Consejo de Edificios Verdes de California (USGBC California) lanzaron el California Building Performance Pulse. Es un panel público y gratuito que muestra el uso de energía, carbono y agua en edificios comerciales y multifamiliares en todo el estado. Incluye datos de más de 1.3 mil millones de pies cuadrados y cubre seis años de información de servicios públicos. El panel permite comparar edificios por ciudad, tipo, tamaño y antigüedad.

**Impacto** – Esta herramienta es única porque combina el uso de agua con datos de energía y carbono, algo que otros paneles públicos no hacen. El uso de agua varía mucho según el tipo de edificio, por eso incluirlo ayuda a los dueños a saber dónde pueden ahorrar agua y energía. Por ejemplo, los hoteles usan mucha más agua por pie cuadrado que las oficinas. El panel ofrece información clara y detallada que dueños, inquilinos y responsables políticos pueden usar para tomar mejores decisiones sobre mejoras y eficiencia. Además, llega justo cuando California se prepara para reglas más estrictas que exigirán que los edificios cumplan con metas de energía y emisiones.

**Próximo paso** – Los dueños pueden usar el panel para ver cómo se comparan sus propiedades y planear mejoras antes de que entren en vigor las nuevas reglas. Los inquilinos pueden preguntar a sus administradores sobre planes de eficiencia. Los responsables políticos pueden usar los datos para crear normas justas y efectivas. A medida que más dueños agreguen sus datos, el panel crecerá y será una herramienta aún más fuerte para mejorar el desempeño de los edificios en California.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo panel público de California ofrece datos claros y combinados sobre el uso de energía, agua y carbono en edificios para ayudar a dueños y responsables a mejorar la eficiencia y cumplir objetivos ambientales futuros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized California cityscape with diverse building silhouettes—commercial towers, apartment blocks, hotels, and hospitals—each glowing softly with color-coded bars or gauges symbolizing energy, water, and carbon performance, interconnected by flowing streams of blue water and green vines representing sustainability and transparency, set against a calm natural sky with gentle earth tones.

articles.title: They pay 30% less for electricity. Their secret? Solar panels
articles.summary: Residents in the town of Escourse, France, pay 30 percent less for their electricity bills than elsewhere in the country. Their secret? Renewable energy. Since 2011, the town has invested more than €1.2 million in solar panels. Homeowners, local businesses and associations now use solar-powered energy. Home to some 800 people, Escourse now produces 60 percent of its own energy, and aims to become 90 percent self-sufficient by 2032.
articles.content:
They pay 30% less for electricity. Their secret? Solar panels

Environment

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again Cover image: DOWN TO EARTH © France 2 03:24

03:24 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

Residents in the town of Escourse, France, pay 30 percent less for their electricity bills than elsewhere in the country. Their secret? Renewable energy. Since 2011, the town has invested more than €1.2 million in solar panels. Homeowners, local businesses and associations now use solar-powered energy. Home to some 800 people, Escourse now produces 60 percent of its own energy, and aims to become 90 percent self-sufficient by 2032.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful and inspiring example of a community achieving significant renewable energy adoption, resulting in lower electricity costs and increased energy self-sufficiency. This has broad environmental and economic benefits and includes sufficient detail about the investment, scale, and future goals.
articles.personality_title: French town cuts electricity bills by 30% with solar power
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Escourse is a small town in France with about 800 people. Like many places, residents used to rely mostly on electricity from outside sources, which could be expensive.

**What happened** – Since 2011, Escourse has invested over €1.2 million in solar panels. These panels are installed on homes, local businesses, and community buildings. Thanks to this effort, the town now produces 60% of its own electricity from solar energy.

**Impact** – Because of the solar panels, residents pay 30% less on their electricity bills compared to other parts of France. This means families and businesses save money every month. Also, producing their own energy helps the town use cleaner power and depend less on outside suppliers.

**What's next step** – Escourse plans to increase its solar energy to cover 90% of its electricity needs by 2032. This will make the town even more independent and could inspire other communities to do the same.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By investing in solar panels, the small town of Escourse has lowered electricity costs and is working toward nearly full energy independence using clean power.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une ville française réduit ses factures d'électricité de 30 % grâce au solaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Escourse est une petite ville en France d'environ 800 habitants. Comme beaucoup d'autres endroits, ses habitants dépendaient surtout de l'électricité fournie de l'extérieur, ce qui pouvait être coûteux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis 2011, Escourse a investi plus de 1,2 million d'euros dans des panneaux solaires. Ces panneaux sont installés sur des maisons, des commerces locaux et des bâtiments communautaires. Grâce à cet effort, la ville produit désormais 60 % de sa propre électricité grâce au solaire.

**Impact** – Grâce aux panneaux solaires, les habitants paient 30 % de moins sur leurs factures d'électricité par rapport à d'autres régions de France. Cela permet aux familles et aux entreprises d'économiser chaque mois. De plus, produire leur propre énergie aide la ville à utiliser une électricité plus propre et à dépendre moins des fournisseurs extérieurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Escourse prévoit d'augmenter la part d'énergie solaire pour couvrir 90 % de ses besoins électriques d'ici 2032. Cela rendra la ville encore plus indépendante et pourrait inspirer d'autres communautés.

**En une phrase** – En investissant dans les panneaux solaires, la petite ville d'Escourse a réduit ses coûts d'électricité et travaille à une quasi-autonomie énergétique avec une énergie propre.
articles.personality_title_es: Pueblo francés reduce su factura eléctrica un 30 % gracias a la energía solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Escourse es un pequeño pueblo en Francia con unas 800 personas. Como muchos lugares, antes dependían principalmente de electricidad de fuentes externas, que podía ser costosa.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2011, Escourse ha invertido más de 1,2 millones de euros en paneles solares. Estos paneles se instalaron en casas, negocios locales y edificios comunitarios. Gracias a esto, el pueblo ahora produce el 60 % de su propia electricidad con energía solar.

**Impacto** – Gracias a los paneles solares, los residentes pagan un 30 % menos en sus facturas de electricidad en comparación con otras partes de Francia. Esto permite que familias y negocios ahorren dinero cada mes. Además, producir su propia energía ayuda al pueblo a usar energía más limpia y depender menos de proveedores externos.

**Próximo paso** – Escourse planea aumentar la energía solar para cubrir el 90 % de sus necesidades eléctricas para 2032. Esto hará que el pueblo sea aún más independiente y podría inspirar a otras comunidades.

**Frase clave** – Al invertir en paneles solares, el pequeño pueblo de Escourse ha reducido sus costos de electricidad y avanza hacia una independencia energética casi total con energía limpia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a quaint French village with rooftops covered in neatly arranged solar panels glowing softly under a gentle sun, surrounded by rolling hills and simple homes connected by glowing energy lines symbolizing community-powered electricity savings.
